title: How Often Should You Clean Your Commercial Space? A Plain-English Guide for Newcastle Businesses
excerpt: Most Newcastle businesses are either overcleaning or undercleaning — and both cost money. This guide breaks down how to work out the right cleaning frequency for your space, your headcount, and your industry.
content:
## The honest answer: it depends, but not on what most people think

When a Newcastle business owner asks how often they should be cleaning their office, the usual answer they get is "five days a week" — because that's what most commercial cleaners default to quoting.

The real answer is more nuanced. Cleaning frequency should match your foot traffic, your industry, your lease obligations, and what your team actually needs to work well. Get it wrong in either direction and you're either wasting money on unnecessary cleans or slowly letting your space become a place people dread coming to.

Here's how to think through it.

## Start with headcount and usage, not habit

The biggest driver of how often a commercial space needs cleaning is how many people are in it and for how long.

A 10-person professional services office in Charlestown with a hybrid work model — say, 60% of staff in on any given day — probably doesn't need five-day-a-week cleaning. Three days might be the right call, with a quick tidy of bathrooms and kitchens on the off days.

A 30-person team in a Hunter Street office doing five days a week in person? That kitchen and those bathrooms need attention every day. No question.

So before you lock in a schedule, map out your actual usage. How many people are in, and on which days? Does foot traffic spike mid-week? Do you have clients or visitors coming through regularly?

## Industry matters more than most people realise

Your industry shapes your cleaning needs in ways that go beyond basic office hygiene.

**Professional services (law, finance, consulting):** Client-facing environments in Newcastle's CBD need to look sharp. Dust on surfaces, marks on glass partitions, or a grubby reception area sends the wrong message. These spaces typically need cleaning three to five days a week depending on client volume.

**Medical and allied health:** If you're running a physio clinic, GP practice, or similar in somewhere like Maitland or Broadmeadow, you're dealing with infection control obligations, not just aesthetics. That usually means daily cleaning with clinical-grade products in treatment areas. Your cleaner needs to understand the difference between regular and compliant.

**Warehouses and industrial:** Less frequent general cleans, but more focus on specific areas — lunchrooms, toilets, entry points. A fortnightly or weekly clean often works for smaller operations.

**Retail and hospitality:** High foot traffic means daily is standard. The question is usually time of day, not frequency.

## The four areas that drive most complaints

Whichever schedule you land on, these four areas cause the most problems when they're not cleaned often enough:

**Bathrooms.** Non-negotiable. Even in a small office, bathrooms need attention at minimum three times a week. Daily is better. This is the one area where most people form their opinion of how well a business is run.

**Kitchen and breakout areas.** Spills, crumbs, dishes — these accumulate fast. A kitchen that gets cleaned once a week in a busy office is a kitchen that smells by Thursday.

**Entry and reception.** First impression. Floors here take more traffic than anywhere else in the building. Factor that into your schedule.

**High-touch surfaces.** Door handles, lift buttons, light switches, shared equipment. These are often missed on a standard clean. If you have staff working through cold and flu season (which in Newcastle is basically May through August), make sure your cleaning spec explicitly covers these.

## When to reassess your current setup

Most businesses set up a cleaning contract when they first move in and don't revisit it. That's usually a mistake.

Your cleaning needs change when:
- Your headcount grows or shrinks
- You move to a hybrid or WFH model
- You take on a new floor or additional space
- You start hosting clients or events more regularly
- Your lease is coming up for renewal (make-good obligations become relevant)

If you signed a contract two years ago and your team has grown from 8 to 20, you're probably undercleaning. If you went fully hybrid last year and still have a five-day schedule, you're overpaying.

## What to actually ask your cleaner

When you're reviewing your cleaning schedule — whether with your current provider or a new one — ask these questions:

- What does a standard clean at our frequency actually include?
- Are bathrooms and kitchens covered every visit?
- What's not included in the base price?
- Can we scale the schedule up or down as our needs change?
- Do you carry public liability and workers' compensation insurance?

If a cleaning company can't answer those questions clearly, that tells you something.

## Getting the balance right

The goal isn't the cheapest possible cleaning schedule or the most thorough one. It's the one that keeps your space in good shape, meets your obligations, and doesn't waste money on cleans that aren't needed.

For most Newcastle businesses, that means taking 20 minutes to actually think through your usage before agreeing to whatever the default quote suggests.

If you want a second opinion on whether your current setup makes sense, the YOS Cleaning team works with businesses across Newcastle, Lake Macquarie, and the Hunter. We'll give you a straight answer, not a sales pitch.
